caveats: a: No source measures a wage-weighted, not-yet-automated AI task share for this industry or narrowed lens.
a: The provided compensation-to-receipts ratio of 0.2427 combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and covers branded, captive, and government-oriented producers as well as contract shops.
rho: The evidence establishes feasible applications and constraints, not a realized five-year implementation rate.
rho: Readiness differs materially between full-service digital OEMs and small job shops with manual records.
e: No denominator study measures the qualifying outsourced-manufacturing share of LMM firms in NAICS 332994.
e: Many component suppliers can be classified as general machine shops, while licensed branded producers can appear in this code despite little external manufacturing revenue.
e: The provided firm count of 99 is estimated from full-code size classes and may double-count establishments or misclassify diversified parents.
s5: Two strategic transactions do not establish a population hazard rate.
s5: Asset purchases that discontinue a brand may preserve manufacturing capability but not all recurring customer relationships.
q: No source directly measures discounted five-year retention of an implemented AI gross benefit.
q: Commercial private-label, branded-component, and defense-program contracts have materially different switching and pricing structures.
d5: Public-brand and ATF production data do not isolate outsourced manufacturing or hold product quality and mix constant.
d5: Political events, regulation, consumer confidence, ammunition availability, and new-product acceptance can move demand rapidly.
o: This is bounded judgment rather than an observed displacement rate.
o: Operator-required demand can persist while moving from an eligible supplier into a customer's captive facility.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Small Arms, Ordnance, and Ordnance Accessories Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers place recurring purchase orders and program releases for machined parts, assemblies, finished or private-label weapons, and accessories. Revenue is generally per component, unit, lot, or production program, with engineering, tooling, qualification, traceability, finishing, testing, and configuration support incorporated into quotes or separately charged.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code mixes proprietary firearm brands, government-oriented weapon producers, accessories, and outsourced OEM manufacturing. The lens is narrowed to contract, build-to-print, OEM, and private-label operators because their repeat external-customer revenue creates a coherent service model; the narrowing is for comparability, not attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Proprietary consumer brands without meaningful outsourced manufacturing revenue; captive manufacturing units; retailers and distributors; gunsmithing and repair; ammunition manufacturing; hobby or one-off custom builds; component work classified outside this code; and firms outside the lower-middle-market control-acquisition band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market OEM, build-to-print, contract, private-label, and high-volume component manufacturers that repeatedly produce small arms, receivers, barrels, actions, suppressors, weapon components, or ordnance accessories for external firearm brands, defense primes, and government customers.

a.bridge: NIST describes AI applications in small and medium manufacturing generally, while contract-manufacturer pages document this lens's engineering, machining, assembly, inspection, traceability, scheduling, and configuration workflows. I map predictive maintenance and quality, scrap reduction, forecasting, quoting, document search, and production planning to the likely wage mix. The bridge likely overstates substitution in physical machining and assembly and understates leverage in fragmented quote, engineering, and quality records.
caveats: No source measures a wage-weighted, not-yet-automated AI task share for this industry or narrowed lens.
The provided compensation-to-receipts ratio of 0.2427 combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and covers branded, captive, and government-oriented producers as well as contract shops.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted occupational roster and task-level time study from several eligible shops, identifying already automated work and time spent in physical, analytical, documentation, and customer-facing tasks, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: AI exposure is meaningful in production scheduling, demand and material forecasting, machine-health analysis, vision-inspection review, quality trends, engineering search, quoting, configuration records, and customer administration. CNC tending, setup, finishing, assembly, test firing, material movement, and licensed physical custody remain substantially hands-on.

d5.bridge: ATF reports licensed domestic firearm production across the whole market, and Ruger and Smith & Wesson describe branded-product unit, demand, inventory, and sourcing dynamics; none measures constant-price year-five demand for outsourced LMM production. I translate the post-peak normalization, continuing product turnover, and ongoing third-party capacity use to approximately flat demand. Branded-market evidence may overstate cyclicality for defense suppliers and understate the effect of customer insourcing on contract shops.
caveats: Public-brand and ATF production data do not isolate outsourced manufacturing or hold product quality and mix constant.
Political events, regulation, consumer confidence, ammunition availability, and new-product acceptance can move demand rapidly.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price OEM purchase volumes, eligible-firm backlog and utilization, customer sourcing plans, AFMER output by manufacturer, distributor inventory, and funded government small-arms programs would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Firearm replacement, new-product cycles, accessories, law-enforcement or defense programs, and brands' desire for flexible external capacity support continued work. Demand is discretionary and politically sensitive, domestic production has declined from a recent peak, customers can rationalize SKUs or insource, and distributor inventories amplify cycles.

caveats: No denominator study measures the qualifying outsourced-manufacturing share of LMM firms in NAICS 332994.
Many component suppliers can be classified as general machine shops, while licensed branded producers can appear in this code despite little external manufacturing revenue.
The provided firm count of 99 is estimated from full-code size classes and may double-count establishments or misclassify diversified parents.
e.change_evidence: A parent-level census combining ATF licensees, AFMER producers, NAICS establishments, CAGE and ITAR records, websites, customer disclosures, revenue bands, and outsourced-revenue share would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Multiple US firms explicitly market OEM, contract, build-to-print, and private-label services, confirming a substantial outsourced segment. Eligibility is reduced by proprietary brands, custom gunsmiths, captive divisions, distributors, firms classified primarily as general machine shops, and companies too small or too large for the control-acquisition band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 332994 ($2,360,444,365) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 332994 ($10,370,911,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $408,191 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332994); class <5: 216 firms, avg revenue/firm $645,889 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $126,723 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 80 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,591,962 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $312,343 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 52 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,071,635 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $602,655 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 99 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,384,364 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,822,212 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 30 firms, avg revenue/firm $79,624,833 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $15,622,392 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $360,677,647 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $70,764,954 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 99

caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than an observed displacement rate.
Operator-required demand can persist while moving from an eligible supplier into a customer's captive facility.
o.change_evidence: A survey of firearm brands and defense customers covering insourcing, additive manufacturing, supplier consolidation, design changes, and five-year sourcing commitments would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: The year-five basket still requires physical machining, forming, finishing, assembly, inspection, testing, serialization, and delivery. Software can improve and coordinate these activities but cannot provide the physical weapon or component or eliminate licensed accountability. Some volume can be insourced, consolidated, or redesigned away, keeping the base below one.

q.bridge: Smith & Wesson is a large branded producer that also sells third-party manufacturing services, and its filings describe purchase-order pricing, promotions, component sourcing, and margin pressure rather than AI-benefit retention by LMM contract shops. I translate those competitive and procurement dynamics to per-part OEM contracts, allowing greater retention for yield, uptime, tooling, engineering, and qualified scarce capacity. The proxy likely understates retention in locked-in defense or serialized programs and overstates it for easily rebid commodity components.
caveats: No source directly measures discounted five-year retention of an implemented AI gross benefit.
Commercial private-label, branded-component, and defense-program contracts have materially different switching and pricing structures.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-firm MSAs, purchase orders, quote histories, open-book provisions, indexation, rebid cadence, tooling ownership, supplier-scorecard data, and margin bridges following productivity projects would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Customers can dual-source and rebid standardized machined parts, while brand and distributor price pressure flows upstream over renewals. Operators retain more when AI benefits reduce scrap, improve machine availability, shorten lead time, prevent quality escapes, or make complex low-volume work economical without appearing as a transparent labor-rate reduction.

rho.bridge: NIST's adoption guidance covers manufacturers broadly, and the industry sources show modern software, quality systems, traceability, technical-data, licensed, and ITAR-controlled workflows rather than actual AI implementation rates. I translate the documented data and process structure to achievable human-in-the-loop deployment, discounted for customer approval, cybersecurity, product liability, quality, and regulatory constraints. This may be low for modern digital machining cells and high for small legacy shops with sparse data.
caveats: The evidence establishes feasible applications and constraints, not a realized five-year implementation rate.
Readiness differs materially between full-service digital OEMs and small job shops with manual records.
rho.change_evidence: Structured interviews with operations, engineering, quality, compliance, and IT leaders at at least ten eligible firms, including data readiness, customer approvals, pilot conversion, validation requirements, and deployment metrics, would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Planning, quoting, maintenance triage, document retrieval, and inspection-exception prioritization can be introduced incrementally. Product configuration, serialized records, controlled technical data, safety-critical dimensions, proof and function testing, and final release require validation and accountable human review.

s5.bridge: Ruger's Anderson asset purchase and Colt CZ's acquisition of Valley Steel Stamp are recent control or operating-asset transfers involving US firearm and component manufacturers, but they do not measure the five-year hazard across the eligible lens. I use them as direct evidence of strategic interest and transferable facilities, workforce, and supplier capability, then widen for unobserved firms. This can overstate turnover because prominent strategic deals are selected and understate it because small private transactions are often undisclosed.
caveats: Two strategic transactions do not establish a population hazard rate.
Asset purchases that discontinue a brand may preserve manufacturing capability but not all recurring customer relationships.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census from PitchBook, CapIQ, GF Data, Axial, firearms advisers, ATF license changes, bankruptcy auctions, and transaction announcements, matched to a verified eligible denominator, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Strategic buyers acquire machining capability, facilities, workforce, supplier relationships, and product capacity, and a fragmented owner base creates possible succession supply. Licensing, product liability, environmental diligence, customer concentration, foreign-ownership review, owner dependence, and cyclical distress can delay or convert sales into piecemeal asset liquidations.

principal_driver: A broad OEM supplier base and recurring precision-manufacturing workflows create tangible AI use cases and acquisition evidence.
principal_weakness: Demand volatility and strong OEM procurement power can erase utilization and savings, while licensing and liability raise implementation and transaction risk.
